coredata.dc:title: Solution-processable carbon dots with efficient solid-state red/near-infrared emission
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Qu, Yanfei
Bai, Xuewei
Li, Di
Zhang, Xiaoyu
Liang, Chao
Zheng, Weitao
Qu, Songnan
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) emerge as promising luminescent materials for potential applications in optoelectronics on basis of their merits including low cost, eco-friendliness and strong, color-tunable photoluminescence (PL). However, the research on solid-state emissive CDs is still at the primary stage because of the aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) of PL and their poor film-formation ability. In this work, we produce CDs with branched-polyethylenimine (b-PEI) chemically functionalized on the surfaces. The thus newly synthesized P-CDs successfully overcome the bottleneck of ACQ effect and display efficient red and NIR emission in aggregate state. Under the excitation of 520 nm, a strong red emission (maxima of 640 nm) with a high photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of 21% was observed for the P-CDs in neat film. Moreover, this design strategy endows the P-CDs with good film-formation ability via solution spin-coating, which significantly increases its value for the film-based optoelectronic devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Red/near-infrared luminescence
Solid-state luminescence
Aggregation-induced luminescence quenching
Solution process

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The morphology characterizations of P-CDs: a) HRTEM image (inset: size distribution of P-CD particles); b) AFM image (inset: height profile along the line in the AFM image).
a) FT-IR spectra of the original CDs and P-CDs; b) 1H nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectrum of P-CDs; c-e) high-resolution XPS spectra for (c) C 1s, (d) N 1s, and (e) O 1s elements of P-CDs.

a) UV–vis absorption spectra of the original CDs in aqueous solution, P-CDs in aqueous solution and film; b) normalized emission spectra of P-CDs in aqueous solution under the excitation of different wavelengths; c) normalized emission spectra of P-CDs in film under the excitation of different wavelengths (inset: photograph of P-CDs film on the quartz substrate under the excitation of UV lamp); d) under 405 nm excitation with fluorescence signals collected at 650 nm. e) Top-view TA data of P-CDs. f) TA spectra of P-CD film at indicated delay times after excitation at 400 nm.
a) AFM image and b) SEM cross-section of the film of P-CDs prepared by solution spin-coating on the silicon wafer; c) the AFM image on the ZnO film (down) compared with ZnO film alone (up).
a) Photograph and b) emission spectrum of the working P-CDs-based deep-red LED (inset: CIE coordinates).
Schematic illustration of the preparation of P-CDs.

head.ce:title: Solution-processable carbon dots with efficient solid-state red/near-infrared emission

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Automobile Materials, College of Materials Science and Engineering, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

Institute of Applied Physics and Materials Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Joint Key Laboratory of the Ministry of Education, Institute of Applied Physics and Materials Engineering, University of Macau, Avenida da Universidade, Taipa 999078, Macau

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) emerge as promising luminescent materials for potential applications in optoelectronics on basis of their merits including low cost, eco-friendliness and strong, color-tunable photoluminescence (PL). However, the research on solid-state emissive CDs is still at the primary stage because of the aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) of PL and their poor film-formation ability. In this work, we produce CDs with branched-polyethylenimine (b-PEI) chemically functionalized on the surfaces. The thus newly synthesized P-CDs successfully overcome the bottleneck of ACQ effect and display efficient red and NIR emission in aggregate state. Under the excitation of 520 nm, a strong red emission (maxima of 640 nm) with a high photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of 21% was observed for the P-CDs in neat film. Moreover, this design strategy endows the P-CDs with good film-formation ability via solution spin-coating, which significantly increases its value for the film-based optoelectronic devices.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), luminescent carbon-based nanoparticles consisting of carbogenic core and surface functional groups, have attracted much attention in recent years.[1-8] In comparison to the conventional luminescent materials such as rare-earth-metals, organic dyes and quantum dots,[9-12] CDs possess the advantages of high photostability, environment-friendliness, low cost, and easy preparation. With these remarkable features, CDs have been regarded as one of the most appealing candidates to replace the conventional luminescent materials for potential applications in optoelectronics.[13-20]

In general, CDs display strong fluorescence in dispersed state (solution or solid matrix), while only faint or no emission in aggregate state (solid film or powders). This phenomenon named as aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) of fluorescence is probably attributed to the nonradiative relaxation induced by the coupled surface states in the aggregates, the resonance energy transfer among particles, and/or π–π interactions between neighboring CDs.[21-23] At the same time, the strong interactions between the abundant surface hydrophilic groups result in poor film-formation ability for CDs via solution-processing.[17,24] Apparently, the quenching of fluorescence in the aggregates and difficulty in solution processing largely limit the application of CDs in solid-state luminescent devices.

In this content, much efforts have been continuously donated to realize efficient solid-state emission for CDs. A typical strategy is embedding CDs in solid state matrices to maintain the photoluminescence (PL) in dispersed state. Consequently, the photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of the composite is concentration-dependent which decreases the repeatability for preparation and flexibility for application.[25] In another way, developing superior CDs with efficient emission in aggregate state are highly desirable to essentially conquer the ACQ of CDs. Liu’s group employed poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA) and ethylenediamine (EDA) as the precursors to synthesize the yellow-green emissive CDs in aggregate state (emission maximum λ em = 525 nm).[26] The surface PVA chains prevented the π–π interactions of graphitizing cores and thus resisted ACQ in solid state. In another way, the same group prepared CDs with rotatable symmetric surfaces (heterocycles around the disulfide bond), which showed blue emission from cores in dispersed state and red aggregation-induced emission in aggregate state.[27] In the aggregates, the blue emission was quenched because of π–π stacking of the conjugated cores, and the red emission (λ em = 621 nm) from surface state occurred due to the restriction of intramolecular rotation of the symmetrical heterocycles around the disulfide bonds. Yang’s group constructed self-quenching-resistant polymer CDs via microwave heating of maleic acid and EDA. These CDs exhibited solid-state fluorescence at 625 nm and QY of 8.5%.[28] Different from CDs with extended conjugation structure, the polymer CDs featured crosslinked polymer network structure with some special groups (named as sub-fluorophores) as fluorescence centers. The suppression of ACQ was because the sub-fluorophores were encapsulated in the network structure of polymer chains and did not undergo π–π interaction process. Fan’s group produced carbon quantum rings featuring stable blue/green/red solid-state fluorescence with PLQYs up to 30–46%.[29] The ring structures suppressed π–π stacking effect in aggregate state and thus benefited the efficient solid-state emission. Another report from Xiong’s group presented boron-doped CDs with red emission peak at 620 nm and PLQY of 18.2% in aggregate state.[30] The solid-state fluorescence was speculated to originate from boron-doped structures in CDs. Employing citric acid and urea as precursors, our group prepared two kinds of self-quenching-resistant CDs exhibiting green solid-state emissions with PLQY of 25–65% through either space-confined vacuum heating synthesis[31] or surface treatment with H2O2.[32] These reports significantly promoted the construction and luminescence study of self-quenching-resistant CDs. However, the species of aggregate-state emissive CDs are still very limited. More importantly, the emission colors of these CDs in aggregate state are mainly located in the blue to yellow spectral region. [26,32-37] The CDs which display efficient aggregate-state red emission are relatively rare, and the aggregate-state near-infrared (NIR) emissive CDs have never been realized up to date.

To develop new CDs displaying efficient aggregate-state red/NIR emission, we pay much attention to suppress the ACQ effect since the ACQ of fluorescence would be much more serious in red to NIR region than in visible range. Herein, we design and synthesize a new kind of red/NIR emissive CDs (P-CDs) with branched-polyethylenimine (b-PEI) chemically functionalized on the surfaces (Scheme 1 ). The design strategy not only makes the surface state of P-CDs homogeneous, but also avoids directly connection of neighboring carbogenic cores, thus significantly suppressing nonradiative relaxation induced by the coupled surface states, as well as π–π interactions between neighboring P-CDs. As a result, the P-CDs display efficient red/NIR emission in aggregate state. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report of CDs displaying NIR emission in aggregate state. Moreover, this design strategy endows the P-CDs with good film-formation ability via solution spin-coating, which is highly desired for the application in film-based optoelectronic devices.

The original CDs which display efficient red emission in solution but non-emission in solid state were prepared according to our recent report.[38] Further reaction of these CDs (10 mg) with b-PEI (molecular weight: 1800; 2 g) was conducted in DMF solution (15 mL) in a solvothermal manner (160 °C, 4 h) (Scheme 1). Next, the reaction mixture was purified by dialyzing (molecular weight: 2000) for 4 days to remove the unreacted b-PEI and other impurities, and the product P-CDs were obtained after freeze-drying.

The morphology and structure of newly synthesized P-CDs have been characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM). As shown in TEM (Fig. S1) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image (Fig. 1 a), the diameters of P-CD particles distribute in the range of 1.5–5 nm, with an average diameter of ∼3 nm; the well-defined lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.21 nm reveal a [110] graphene plane in P-CDs.[39,40] As for the AFM image (Fig. 1b), the height of P-CDs could be ensured in the range of 2–5 nm, which is consistent with the TEM image.

In the FT-IR spectrum of P-CDs, the strong absorption peak at 3420 cm−1 and the wide absorption band at 400–800 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration and the out-of-plane vibration of NH, respectively (Fig. 2 a). The very strong and sharp peak at 1662 cm−1, which is a characteristic absorption of CO stretching vibration of amide, is also observed for P-CDs.[41] Meanwhile, P-CDs display significantly weaker absorption in the range of 2500–3000 cm−1 than the original CDs except the symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibration of CH in the b-PEI chains at 2840 and 2950 cm−1, demonstrating the decrease of the COOH moieties.[42] These results reveal that the NH2 groups of b-PEI have been chemically condensed with the COOH moieties of the original CDs during the formation of P-CDs. The 1H nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectrum of P-CDs (Fig. 2b) presents the signals of protons in aromatic rings (7.8–8.05 ppm), and the methylene protons (2.3–3.5 ppm). Comparing with the peaks of pristine b-PEI precursors (2.3–2.7 ppm, inset in Fig. 2b), the signals (2.7–3.6 ppm) in downfield are probably assigned to protons in methylene attached to the amide groups and in b-PEI segments chemically bonded on CDs.[43-45] It indicates that b-PEI has been chemically reacted with the original CDs to produce the new P-CDs composed of sp2 domains and PEI moieties. The chemical bonding information of P-CDs has been further revealed by the XPS measurements (Fig. S2). The high-resolution C 1s (Fig. 2c), N 1s (Fig. 2d), O 1s (Fig. 2e) XPS spectra confirm the existence of aromatic carbon (283.6 eV), CC (284.4 eV), CN (285.2 eV), and CO (287 eV) for C; CNC (398.2 eV), amide (399 eV), and amine (399.8 eV) for N; CO (530.5 eV) and CO (532 eV) for O.[46-49] The element analysis identifies the C, N, and O contents of 46.8%, 22.9% and 21.2% (calculated) for P-CDs.

The photophysical property of P-CDs has been comprehensively investigated in both solution and solid state. In aqueous solution, P-CDs display two strong absorption bands with the maxima (λ abs) at 336 nm and 540 nm (Fig. 3 a). The absorption band at 540 nm attributed to the carbogenic core of P-CDs is also observed in the original CDs. Notably, the broad absorption band at long wavelength around 700 nm, which is quite strong in the original CDs and corresponding to the surface state, has been dramatically decreased in the P-CDs.[38] This result is reasonable since the surface of original CDs has been chemically functionalized with b-PEI and indicates the surface groups of P-CDs are more homogeneous.[32,38] In comparison to the solution, the absorption spectrum of P-CD film shows a broader absorption band with maximum at around 330 nm and 550 nm with a shoulder at 575 nm.

The emission spectra of P-CDs were measured in next and the excitation wavelength-dependent emissions were observed. Under the excitation of 520 nm, the P-CDs aqueous solution displays intense red emission with the maximum (λ em) of 614 nm (Fig. 3b). Upon shifting the excitation to 660 nm and 690 nm, the λ em of P-CDs solution is moved to 670 nm and 705 nm, respectively. The emission peak located at ∼614 nm is originated from the carbogenic core, as this emission is nearly independent of the excitation wavelengths in spectral range of 380–580 nm (Fig. S3a), and has no obvious change comparing with the core state fluorescence of the original CDs (Fig. S4). On the other hand, the longer wavelength emission centers at 670 nm and 705 nm should be corresponding to the surface states of P-CDs. In aggregate state, the P-CDs display a similar fluorescent behavior with that in solution. Under the excitation of 520 nm, 660 nm and 690 nm, the λ em of P-CD film is located at 640 nm, 670 nm and 715 nm, respectively (Fig. 3c), and the red emission band at ∼640 nm is also excitation-wavelength-independent (Fig. S3b). The emission of P-CDs film with λ em of 715 nm (with a photo in Fig. S5) is impressive and represents the first example of CDs which display NIR emission in aggregate state. Notably, both excitation (690 nm) and emission (715 nm) wavelengths are in the NIR region. Even if with a small Stokes shift (25 nm) for the P-CDs film, the relative wide emission spectrum enables to maintain strong NIR emission at around 750 nm or even longer wavelength under the excitation of 690 nm. More importantly, the red/NIR emissions of the P-CDs are efficient in film: the PLQY is 21% under the excitation of 520 nm and is 5% under the excitation of 660 nm (Fig. S6).

The PLQY of P-CDs film is very near to that of the dilute solution (19% under the excitation of 520 nm and 4% under the excitation of 660 nm in ethanol), indicating that the ACQ effect has been efficiently suppressed by the design strategy of surface chemical functionalization with b-PEI. To deeply understand the photo-excited process occurring in P-CDs, femtosecond (fs) transient absorption (TA) spectroscopy and PL lifetime measurement have been carried out. Upon fs pulse excitation at 400 nm, the TA spectrum of P-CD film (Fig. 3e-f) exhibits a negative band of ground-state bleaching (GSB) at ∼580 nm and a positive band of excited-state absorption (ESA) at ∼750 nm. The GSB decay time constant at 580 nm is 1840 ps (96%), which is close to that of 1990 ps (95%) at 750 nm for ESA (Fig. S7). The decay processes in nanosecond scale correspond to the radiative transitions and their high proportions up to around 95% agree with the efficient emission of the P-CD film. The total energy of 2.1 eV (580 nm) and 1.7 eV (750 nm) reaches 3.8 eV, which matches the energy of the optical absorption band at around 330 nm of P-CD film (Fig. 3a). The fluorescence lifetimes (τ) of P-CDs were measured both in solution and film under 405 nm excitation with fluorescence signals collected at 650 nm. As shown in Fig. 3d, the fluorescence decay profiles of ethanol solution and film are similar with each other. On basis of the fitting of the decay profiles, the lifetimes of solution and film are determined to be 3.12 ns and 2.81 ns, respectively. With the PLQY and τ values in hand, the radiative (k r) and nonradiative (k nr) decay rate constants are calculated (using equations S1 and S2): k r = 0.61 × 108 s−1 and k nr = 2.6 × 108 s−1 for solution, k r = 0.75 × 108 s−1 and k nr = 2.8 × 108 s−1 for film. The k r and k nr values of film are near to those of solution, revealing that the excited state characters of them are similar with each other. In other words, the excited state character of P-CDs in solution would be maintained in film because of the branched polymers on the surface. In comparison to other solid-state red-emissive CDs, e.g. the CDs (k r = 0.20 × 108 s−1, k nr = 1.9 × 108 s−1 calculated from reported PLQY of 9.6% and PL lifetime of 4.82 ns) with emission maximum at 640 nm[48] and the carbon nanorings (k r = 0.24 × 108 s−1, k nr = 0.56 × 108 s−1 calculated from reported PLQY of 30% and PL lifetime of 12.5 ns) with PL peak at 630 nm,[29] P-CDs display larger k r value (about three times) which directly contributes to the efficient solid-state emission. On one hand, the surface moieties of P-CDs dominated by NH2 groups are more homogeneous comparing with those of original CDs, which can eliminate coupled surface states in the aggregates; on the other hand, the π–π interactions between neighboring CD fluorescent centers can be avoided owing to the steric hindrance of backbones and side chains of b-PEI. As a result, the ACQ effect could be significantly suppressed and the considerable PLQY could be expected in solid state.

The b-PEI chemically functionalized surfaces not only ensure intense solid-state emission of P-CDs in red/NIR region, but also endow them with good film-formation ability on hydrophilic substrate. In general, the solution spin-coating of small molecules or polymers could easily provide smooth films. In contrast, the spin-coating of CDs to fabricate films is usually not so successful because of the serious aggregation of nanoparticles induced by strong interactions of the surface groups such as hydrogen bonds during the spin-coating process. This problem has directly limited the application of CDs in film-based optoelectronic devices. Here, the b-PEI polymers on the surface of P-CDs efficiently suppress the aggregation of nanoparticles and facilitate film formation. The spin-coating of EtOH solution of P-CDs (30 mg/mL) can provide smooth and thin films on kinds of hydrophilic substrates such as silicon wafers, quartz plates, and ZnO films. As shown in the AFM and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images (Fig. 4 and Fig. S8), the film of P-CDs on silicon wafer is smooth, thin (about 50 nm) and the thickness is uniform. The roughness of film characterized by AFM image is only 0.311 nm. Meanwhile, the photographs of CD film spin-coated on quartz plate (inset of Fig. 3c) and its confocal fluorescence microscopy image (Fig. S9) further prove that the film is quite smooth in a wide area with uniform fluorescence. The film formation property of the original CDs has also been studied for comparison. Film of the original CDs was prepared via spin-coating the aqueous solution of original CDs with concentration of 5 mg/mL on the quartz substrates. The spin-coating condition (speed and time) was same as that of the preparation of P-CDs film. As shown in the microscopy image (Fig. S10), the film of the original CDs consists of many aggregated particles. The strong aggregation of original CDs should be ascribed to the intense intermolecular hydrogen bonds formed by the abundant carboxyl groups on CD surface. To evaluate the application potential in optoelectronic devices, the film-formation ability of P-CDs on ZnO substrate, which is widely used as electron transporting layer in optoelectronic devices, has been studied. Spin-coating the EtOH solution of P-CDs on ZnO film (composed of ZnO nanoparticles, roughness: ∼10.6 nm) can also produce smooth film with roughness of ∼1 nm, as shown in the AFM image (Fig. 4c). The efficient aggregate-state emission in red/NIR region and good film-formation ability promise P-CDs as potential light emitting materials in solution-processed optoelectronic devices.

Considering the efficient red/NIR emission in solid state, the P-CDs have been preliminarily used as long-wavelength phosphors in the light-emitting devices. Depositing P-CDs on the commercially available InGaN LED chip (UV, 365 nm) provided a deep-red to NIR emitting LED chip with emission band in spectral range of 580–940 nm, CIE color coordinate of (0.57, 0.29) and CCT of 5545 K (Fig. 5 ). The LED displaying significantly longer emission maximum than the film used for the photophysical study is attributed to the reabsorption effect.[50,51] A white-emitting LED chip was also fabricated by depositing the red-emissive P-CDs and the Silicate S525 green-emission phosphors on the blue-emissive InGaN LED chip (460 nm) with CIE color coordinate of (0.31, 0.37) (Fig. S11). In addition, the PL spectrum of P-CDs film was not degraded upon heating the film at 50 °C for 7 h (Fig. S12a). Serving as the phosphors of LED, the P-CDs film also displayed almost consistent PL spectra during continuously working for 7 h (Fig. S12b). These results reveal the good thermal and photo stability of P-CDs, which are highly desired for the optic/optoelectronic application.

We have demonstrated an efficient design strategy to suppress the aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching of CDs that is constructing CDs with branched polymers (b-PEI) chemically functionalized on the surface. Thanks to the homogeneous surface groups and the steric hindrance provided by b-PEI, the thus newly synthesized P-CDs successfully overcome the bottleneck of fluorescence quenching and display efficient red and NIR emission in both solution and solid state. Moreover, this design strategy endows the P-CDs with good film-formation ability via solution spin-coating. Further extending this design strategy to develop new CDs with various emission colors and exploring their application as emitting layers in solution-processed electroluminescence diodes are ongoing in our lab.

Yanfei Qu: Methodology, Investigation, Validation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Xuewei Bai: Methodology, Investigation, Validation, Data curation. Di Li: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Xiaoyu Zhang: Methodology, Validation. Chao Liang: Investigation, Data curation. Weitao Zheng: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration. Songnan Qu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (61975200, 61922091 and 51972136), Jilin Province Science and Technology Research Projects (20180101190JC and 20170101191JC), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities, JLU, the Science and Technology Development Fund of Macau SAR (0040/2019/A1 and FDCT/0073/2019/AMJ), and Fund from the University of Macau (SRG2019-00163-IAPME).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2021.12.192.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrophoretic analysis and purification of fluorescent single-walled carbon nanotube fragments

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X.Y. Xu; R. Ray; Y.L. Gu; H.J. Ploehn; L. Gearheart; K. Raker; W.A. Scrivens, Electrophoretic analysis and purification of fluorescent single-walled carbon nanotube fragments. J. Am. Chem. Soc. 2004, 126 (40), 12736-12737.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Quantum-sized carbon dots for bright and colorful photoluminescence

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.P. Sun; B. Zhou; Y. Lin; W. Wang; K.A.S. Fernando; P. Pathak; M.J. Meziani; B.A. Harruff; X. Wang; H.F. Wang; P.J.G. Luo; H. Yang; M.E. Kose; B.L. Chen; L.M. Veca; S.Y. Xie, Quantum-sized carbon dots for bright and colorful photoluminescence. J. Am. Chem. Soc. 2006, 128 (24), 7756-7757.
A.B. Bourlinos; A. Stassinopoulos; D. Anglos; R. Zboril; M. Karakassides; E.P. Giannelis, Surface functionalized carbogenic quantum dots. Small 2008, 4 (4), 455-458.
G. Ragazzon; A. Cadranel; E.V. Ushakova; Y. Wang; D.M. Guldi; A.L. Rogach; N.A. Kotov; M. Prato, Optical processes in carbon nanocolloids. Chem 2021, 7 (3), 606-628.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon quantum dots and their applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.Y. Lim; W. Shen; Z. Gao, Carbon quantum dots and their applications. Chem. Soc. Rev. 2015, 44 (1), 362-381.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon Dots: A New Type of Carbon-Based Nanomaterial with Wide Applications

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Optical Properties of Carbon Dots in the Deep-Red to Near-Infrared Region Are Attractive for Biomedical Applications

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Red, Green, and Blue Luminescence by Carbon Dots: Full-Color Emission Tuning and Multicolor Cellular Imaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Jiang; S. Sun; L. Zhang; Y. Lu; A. Wu; C. Cai; H. Lin, Red, Green, and Blue Luminescence by Carbon Dots: Full-Color Emission Tuning and Multicolor Cellular Imaging. Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 2015, 54 (18), 5360-5363.
M. Liu; N. Yazdani; M. Yarema; M. Jansen; V. Wood; E.H. Sargent, Colloidal quantum dot electronics. Nat. Electron. 2021, 4 (8), 548-558.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent Labeling of Biomolecules with Organic Probes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.S.T. Gonçalves, Fluorescent Labeling of Biomolecules with Organic Probes. Chem. Rev. 2009, 109 (1), 190-212.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Plasmonic Dual-Enhancement and Precise Color Tuning of Gold Nanorod@SiO2 Coupled Core–Shell–Shell Upconversion Nanocrystals

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Kang; J. He; T. Sun; Z.Y. Bao; F. Wang; D.Y. Lei, Plasmonic Dual-Enhancement and Precise Color Tuning of Gold Nanorod@SiO2 Coupled Core–Shell–Shell Upconversion Nanocrystals. Adv. Funct. Mater. 2017, 27 (36), 1701842.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances and prospects of persistent luminescent materials as inner secondary self-luminous light source for photocatalytic applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Kang; G. Sun; P. Boutinaud; H. Wu; F.-X. Ma; J. Lu; J. Gan; H. Bian; F. Gao; S. Xiao, Recent advances and prospects of persistent luminescent materials as inner secondary self-luminous light source for photocatalytic applications. Chem. Eng. J. 2021, 403, 126099.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Solvent-Controlled Synthesis of Highly Luminescent Carbon Dots with a Wide Color Gamut and Narrowed Emission Peak Widths

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Ding; J.S. Wei; P. Zhang; Z.Y. Zhou; Q.Y. Gao; H.M. Xiong, Solvent-Controlled Synthesis of Highly Luminescent Carbon Dots with a Wide Color Gamut and Narrowed Emission Peak Widths. Small 2018, 14 (22), e1800612.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: 53% Efficient Red Emissive Carbon Quantum Dots for High Color Rendering and Stable Warm White-Light-Emitting Diodes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Wang; F. Yuan; X. Li; Y. Li; H. Zhong; L. Fan; S. Yang, 53% Efficient Red Emissive Carbon Quantum Dots for High Color Rendering and Stable Warm White-Light-Emitting Diodes. Adv. Mater. 2017, 29 (37), 1702910.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots in zeolites: A new class of thermally activated delayed fluorescence materials with ultralong lifetimes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.C. Liu; N. Wang; Y. Yu; Y. Yan; H.Y. Zhang; J.Y. Li; J.H. Yu, Carbon dots in zeolites: A new class of thermally activated delayed fluorescence materials with ultralong lifetimes. Sci. Adv. 2017, 3 (5), e1603171.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots—Emerging light emitters for bioimaging, cancer therapy and optoelectronics
